title: $Λ$-enhanced gray-molasses loading and EIT cooling of neutral atoms in nanophotonic traps
authors: Lucas Pache, Antoine Glicenstein, Philipp Schneeweiss, Jürgen Volz, Arno Rauschenbeutel, Riccardo Pennetta
abstract: Nanophotonic traps for cold atoms typically have trap volumes that are orders of magnitude smaller than, e.g., free-space optical tweezers. This makes efficient loading of these traps challenging, thereby limiting the total number of atoms coupled to the nanophotonic waveguide. Here, we demonstrate that $Λ$-enhanced gray-molasses ($Λ$GM) can substantially increase the number of trapped atoms in a nanofiber-based cold-atom setup. Specifically, we observe a six-fold increase in the number of loaded atoms compared to conventional red-detuned polarization gradient cooling. Despite the unusually small depth of our optical trap of only 24 $μ$K, we load about 4000 individual Cesium atoms, achieving optical depths exceeding 140 and reaching the collisional blockade regime over a length of approximately 1 mm. After loading, we perform efficient EIT-assisted cooling that is found to increase the trap storage time to 400(9) ms. This is a 5-fold improvement over the passive storage time. Remarkably, EIT-cooling also works with two co-propagating nanofiber-guided light fields and requiries only about a few hundred picowatt of optical power. Our results provide an efficient method to boost both the number of loaded atoms and the storage time of nanophotonic atom traps.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method to significantly boost the number of atoms and their storage time in nanophotonic traps using advanced cooling techniques. By applying Lambda-enhanced gray-molasses and EIT cooling, the researchers achieved a six-fold increase in atom loading and a five-fold increase in trap lifetime. This provides a robust pathway for improving the optical depth and coupling strength in nanofiber-based quantum interfaces.
main_problem: Efficiently loading neutral atoms into nanophotonic traps is difficult due to extremely small trap volumes, which limits the number of atoms coupled to the waveguide and their storage time.
main_result: The implementation of Lambda-enhanced gray-molasse (LambdaGM) cooling leads to a six-fold increase in the number of loaded Cesium atoms and, combined with EIT cooling, extends the trap storage time by up to five-fold.
method: The researchers use a sequential cooling approach starting with Red-detuned Polarization Gradient Cooling (RPGC) followed by Lambda-enhanced gray-molasses (LambdaGM) on the D1 transition, and subsequently apply EIT cooling using fiber-guided light fields.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of an optical nanofiber-based dipole trap using the evanescent field of a tapered optical fiber to trap Cesium atoms in a two-color (red and blue detuned) configuration.
key_observables: Optical depth (OD), atom number (N), atom-photon coupling strength (beta), atomic temperature, trap storage time, and loading/loss rates.
important_parameters: Trap depth of 24 microK, trap volume of approximately 1e-16 cm^3, EIT cooling power at the picowatt scale, and the collisional blockade regime.
figures_summary: Fig 1a shows the experimental setup; Fig 2 shows optimal two-photon detuning and OD; Fig 3 shows fluorescence imaging; Fig 5 shows comparison of atom storage times and the observation of a Fano-type resonance in OD.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the nanophotonic trapping challenge, describes the experimental setup and cooling sequence (MOT, RPGC, LambdaGM, EIT), presents results on loading enhancement and atom numbers, demonstrates storage time extension via EIT, and concludes with the performance of fiber-guided cooling.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates highly efficient sub-Doppler cooling techniques (LambdaGM and EIT) specifically optimized for the extreme constraints of nanophotonic interfaces, which is crucial for developing scalable quantum networks and light-matter interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Experimental setup for our nanofiber-based cold atom interface. We prepare a cloud of cold cesium atoms close to a 225 nm-waist-radius nanofiber by using a 3D-MOT and molasses configuration with σ+and σ−polarization of the counter-propagating light fields. We trap the atoms close to the surface of the nanofiber using a fiber-guided red-detuned light field (λred = 935 nm) in a standing wave configuration and a fiber-guided blue-detuned light field (λblue = 685 nm) in a running wave configuration. We use fiber-guided light fields on the D2 transition (λgreen = 852 nm) to probe the atoms and to perform EIT-cooling. b) Energy level scheme of the transitions used for molasses cooling:...

FIG. 2. Measurement of the OD of the trapped atomic ensem- ble for different loading times. During the red-shaded time interval, the atoms are loaded using the RPGC-molasses for up to 50.0 ms when they reach a steady state OD of 20.4±1.2. During the blue-shaded time interval, the atoms are loaded further using the ΛGM-molasse. During this second stage, we measure an increase in the OD by a factor of six with a max- imum of 125 ± 4. Lines: Exponential fits to the data. Inset: Measurement of the final OD in relation to the maximally measured OD for different two-photon detuning ∆2P. The dependence is well described by a Gaussian distribution with a maximum close to the two-photon resonance...

FIG. 3. a) EMCCD-fluorescence image recorded while prob- ing with fiber-guided light. b) In conjunction with an inde- pendent atom number measurement, we determine the local filling factor of the trapped ensemble. We observe a flat- topped atom distribution with a maximum filling factor of about 50% , which indicates trap operation in the collisional blockade regime.

FIG. 4. a) Measurement of the storage time of the trapped atoms after loading: The blue curve shows a measurement of the passive storage time of τp = 79 ± 2 ms of the atoms in the nanofiber-guided dipole trap. The red curve shows the active storage time of τa = 400 ± 9 ms when the atoms are cooled by the ΛGM after 45 ms of delay. For the orange curve, the ΛGM-cooling light fields are switched on after a delay of 195 ms. b) Measurement of the atom-light coupling strength β for different holding times normalized by the pas- sive storage time τp: For a non-cooled atomic ensemble (pur- ple) β decreases due to heating of the atoms. When applying the ΛGM-cooling light fields (green) the...

FIG. 5. a) Measurement of the storage time of the trapped atoms after loading when applying no-cooling (blue) and the EIT-cooling light fields through the fiber (red). The blue curve is an exponential fit that yields a passive storage time of τp = 50 ± 1 ms of the atoms in the nanofiber-guided dipole trap. The red curve yields an active storage time of τa = 131 ± 9 ms when the atoms are cooled with the fiber- guided EIT-cooling light fields after 41 ms of delay. b) We observe a Fano-type resonance in OD after a cooling duration of 20 ms when the EIT light fields are scanned across the two- photon resonance ∆2P,EIT c) We find the maximum OD after applying EIT-cooling for a power of the...
